query: paradoxical excitement in children caused by morphine

retrieved_docs.title: Morphine -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Driving and operating machinery: Patients should be warned that morphine may impair their ability to perform activities requiring mental or physical alertness, such as driving or operating machinery, especially when combined with other CNS depressants. Child safety: Morphine must be kept out of children's reach. Accidental ingestion can lead to life-threatening consequences, and immediate medical attention is necessary if it occurs. Opioid-induced hyperalgesia/allodynia: Opioid-induced hyperalgesia (OIH) occurs when opioid use leads to an increased sensitivity to pain, opposite of the expected analgesic effect. Symptoms may include heightened pain with dose escalation, reduced pain with dose reduction, or pain from non-painful stimuli (allodynia), often without evidence of disease progression or opioid withdrawal. If suspected, consideration should be given to dose reduction or opioid rotation to manage OIH and avoid further complications. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Procedural Sedation -- Technique or Treatment -- Pharmacological Agents
retrieved_docs.content: Reversal agents: Reversal agents are essential in managing the effects of overdoses or excessive sedation caused by opioids and benzodiazepines. Naloxone and flumazenil are commonly used to rapidly counteract the effects of these substances, restoring normal respiratory and neurological function.

retrieved_docs.title: Opioid Toxicity -- History and Physical -- Neurological assessment
retrieved_docs.content: Opiates can lower the seizure threshold, potentially leading to generalized seizures, particularly in young children. This effect is mainly due to the paradoxical excitation of the brain. The opiates most commonly involved in adults experiencing seizures are propoxyphene and meperidine. Hearing loss may occur in rare cases, particularly in individuals who have consumed alcohol with heroin. However, this auditory deficit is usually reversible.

retrieved_docs.title: Withdrawal Syndromes -- Etiology -- Opioids
retrieved_docs.content: Opioids exert their pain control effect via secondary messengers, specifically G proteins. After binding to the opioid receptor, the neuron is hyperpolarized and inhibited. With chronic opioid use, the opioid receptor has decreased effectiveness in its ability to open potassium channels, leading to decreased efficacy in hyperpolarization and inhibition of the neuron. In response, there is upregulation of cyclic adenosine monophosphate-mediated responses, which are responsible for neuronal excitability. Upon the discontinuation of an opioid, there is uninhibited activity within the locus ceruleus and decreased activity of inhibitory GABA activity, leading to unopposed counterregulatory excitability. [17] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: West Virginia Opioid Prescribing for Chronic Pain While Avoiding Drug Diversion -- Epidemiology -- National
retrieved_docs.content: Opioids are the most used therapeutic agent for chronic pain and are derived synthetically from generally unrelated compounds. Opiates are derived from the liquid of the opium poppy either by direct refinement or by relatively minor chemical modifications. Both opioids and opiates act on three major classes of opioid receptors: mu, kappa, delta, and several minor classes of opioid receptors like nociceptin and zeta. Simplifying significantly, the mu receptors are thought to provide analgesia, respiratory suppression, bradycardia, physical dependence, gastrointestinal dysmotility, and euphoria. The kappa agonism can yield hallucinations, miosis, and dysphoria. The delta receptor likely has pain control and mood modulation effects, but some have suggested that mu agonism is necessary for the delta receptor to function strongly for analgesia. [27] [28] The nociceptin receptor modulates brain dopamine levels and has clinical effects like analgesia and anxiolysis. The zeta receptor, also known as the opioid growth factor receptor, can modulate certain types of cell proliferation, such as skin growths, and are not thought to have many functions in modulating pain or emotion. [29] [30]

retrieved_docs.content: Partial opioid agonist/synthetics: direct suppression of CNS respiratory center (one lick can be enough to be toxic in a child) [6]

retrieved_docs.title: West Virginia Opioid Prescribing for Chronic Pain While Avoiding Drug Diversion -- History and Physical -- History
retrieved_docs.content: Nonverbal children with neurologic impairment (NI) is a challenging population to assess pain. Caregivers are often needed to help determine changes in the patient's behavior. Grimacing, moaning, increased muscle tone, crying, arching, atypical behavior such as aggressive behavior are a few symptoms to monitor in this population. Nonverbal children with NI include the Revised Face, Legs, Activity, Cry, Consolability (r-FLACC) scale, and the Individualized Numeric Rating Scale (INRS). The assessment adds specific behavior for atypical presentations. [55] [58]

retrieved_docs.title: Opioid Equivalency -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Opioids may also exhibit neuroexcitatory effects that might not be easily recognized. Myoclonus is typically a herald symptom. Myoclonus, which is the uncontrollable jerking and twitching of muscles/muscle groups, most frequently occurs in the extremities. Higher opioid doses may cause myoclonus more frequently, but the dose relationship is variable. [11] As myoclonus worsens, patients may develop other neuroexcitatory signs, including hyperalgesia (increased sensitivity to noxious stimuli), delirium with hallucinations, and grand mal seizures. Treatment of neuroexcitatory effects involves decreasing the opioid dose, opioid rotation, or the addition of a benzodiazepine.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydrocodone -- Toxicity -- Management of Overdose
retrieved_docs.content: Opioid receptors are G-protein-coupled receptors. Agonist stimulation inhibits cAMP formation, inhibiting the release of nociceptive neurotransmitters, postsynaptic neuronal hyperpolarization, and reduced neuronal excitability. [5] [6] [7] Naloxone, a highly competitive mu-receptor antagonist, reverses the inhibition of cAMP formation and nociceptive neurotransmitter release and reverses postsynaptic neuronal hyperpolarization, reducing neuronal excitability. This reversal extends to clinical signs and symptoms of opioid toxicity, including respiratory depression and decreased level of consciousness.

retrieved_docs.title: West Virginia Opioid Prescribing for Chronic Pain While Avoiding Drug Diversion -- Pathophysiology -- Pain Modulation and Transmission
retrieved_docs.content: Peripheral and nerves at other levels of the central nervous system are sensitized, resulting in long-term synaptic changes in cortical receptive fields that can result in exaggerated pain perception. This process of chronic afferent input causing increased sensitivity and lower pain thresholds, with the remodeling of the central nociceptive pathways and receptors termed central sensitization.  The consequence may be: Allodynia (exaggerated response to a nonpainful stimulus) Hyperalgesia (excessive pain response to a normal pain stimulus)

retrieved_docs.title: NP Safe Prescribing of Controlled Substances While Avoiding Drug Diversion -- Epidemiology -- Opioids
retrieved_docs.content: Opioids are the most used therapeutic agent for chronic pain and are derived synthetically from generally unrelated compounds. Opiates are derived from the liquid of the opium poppy either by direct refinement or by relatively minor chemical modifications. Both opioids and opiates act on 3 major classes of opioid receptors: mu, kappa, delta, and several minor classes of opioid receptors like nociceptin and zeta. Simplifying significantly, the mu receptors are thought to provide analgesia, respiratory suppression, bradycardia, physical dependence, gastrointestinal dysmotility, and euphoria. The kappa agonism can yield hallucinations, miosis, and dysphoria. The delta receptor likely has pain control and mood modulation effects, but some have suggested that mu agonism is necessary for the delta receptor to function strongly for analgesia. [20] [21] The nociceptin receptor modulates brain dopamine levels and has clinical effects like analgesia and anxiolysis. The zeta receptor, also known as the opioid growth factor receptor, can modulate certain types of cell proliferation, such as skin growths, and is not thought to have many functions in modulating pain or emotion. [22] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Morphine -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Morphine is a highly beneficial medication when used appropriately. However, in certain situations, this medication may be strongly contraindicated. Extreme caution is necessary for patients with severe respiratory depression and asthma exacerbation cases since morphine can further decrease the respiratory drive. Additionally, morphine should be avoided in cases of previous hypersensitivity reactions and immediately discontinued in the presence of an active reaction. [35] Caution is also necessary with the concurrent use of monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs) as these medications have an additive effect with morphine. This combination can then trigger severe hypotension, serotonin syndrome, or increased respiratory depression in patients. GI obstruction is another important contraindication. [1] One contraindication for opioid administration is patients with a history of substance misuse, especially if a patient has had a history of abusing opioids. Although this is a very controversial topic, most clinicians would agree that pain requires management. [36] However, most will agree and acknowledge that there are alternatives to opioid analgesics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: The antiepileptic therapeutic class (EPC) comprises a broad spectrum of agents used to treat الصرع (epilepsy), including subtypes such as benign neonatal epilepsy, and aims to prevent severe neurological sequelae such as coma, stupor, and brain damage. Core EPC members include valproate (and its calcium, sodium, magnesium salts and related derivatives), lamotrigine, tiagabine (including tiagabine hydrochloride), ethotoin, phenytoin (phenytoin sodium, mephenytoin), ethosuximide, felbamate, fosphenytoin (fosphenytoin sodium), gabapentin enacarbil, lacosamide, levetiracetam, methsuximide, oxcarbazepine, primidone, trimethadione, topiramate, vigabatrin, zonisamide, and others. These drugs exert anticonvulsant effects through distinct mechanisms, allowing clinicians to tailor seizure control to individual patients and specific seizure types. Polypharmacy considerations are highlighted by observed co‑occurrence patterns: dextropropoxyfen frequently co‑occurs with phenytoin, and phenytoin often co‑occurs with the opioid analgesic oxycodone, underscoring the need for vigilance regarding drug‑drug interactions. Oxygen deprivation (hypoxia) is closely associated with the same critical outcomes—coma, stupor, and irreversible brain injury—because insufficient cerebral oxygen supply can precipitate a spectrum of unconscious states and permanent neuronal damage. Recognizing the interrelationship between epilepsy, its antiepileptic treatments, hypoxic events, and the potential for severe neurological impairment is essential for individualized therapy, careful monitoring of concomitant medications, and timely intervention to prevent lasting neurological dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1502
retrieved_docs.content: The physiological effects of drugs encompass a range of pharmacologic actions, and one specific category within this broader group is central nervous system stimulants. Central nervous system stimulants are agents that produce stimulating effects on the brain and spinal cord, influencing alertness, mood, cognition, and energy levels. Recognizing that central nervous system stimulants are a child concept of the overall physiological effects of drugs helps clinicians understand their mechanisms of action, therapeutic indications, and potential adverse effects, thereby supporting more informed prescribing, monitoring, and patient‑care decisions.

Opioid‑related psychiatric and behavioral disorders, including opioid withdrawal states, are connected to the broader substance‑related disorder category. Poisoning and its sequelae form a parallel branch. “Disorder due to and following poisoning” is classified as a type of poisoning (التسمم) and specifically as poisoning by non‑medicinal substances, illustrating that toxic exposures beyond therapeutic drugs are included. This branch links alcoholic poisoning (التسمم الكحولي), drug‑induced levo‑fibrinogen degradation poisoning (تسمم بالأدوية المؤشرة على انحلال الليفين), and adrenal syndrome (متلازمة الأدرينالية). The category “Poisonings and Drug Effects” is positioned under both poisoning and drug dependence, reflecting the overlap between toxic exposures and substance‑related health effects.
